title: Torn-paper collage background
subtitle: Layering torn papers with diluted PVA to build a textured scrapbook background
excerpt: A torn-paper collage background, layers of different papers torn into irregular pieces and adhered with diluted PVA, produces a textured, multi-layered surface that sets off photographs and lettering far more effectively than plain card. The texture comes from the torn edges and from the layering itself. Different papers in the same colour range create depth without clutter.
sourceNotes: Torn-paper collage techniques appear continuously in twentieth-century paper crafts. The technique draws on the papier-mâché and décollage traditions documented in craft manuals from the nineteenth century onward.

glossaryTerms.definition: A layered paper surface built by adhering torn or cut paper pieces to a base sheet. In scrapbooking, the collage background provides visual texture and interest beneath photographs, journalling blocks, and embellishments.
A diluted adhesive used to adhere paper layers and to seal the finished surface in one product. Diluted PVA (1 part water to 2 parts PVA) serves the same function at lower cost.
The feathered, soft-fibre edge produced by tearing paper rather than cutting it. Torn edges integrate more naturally than cut edges when layering paper, as they fade into the adjacent layer.

content.text: depends on the papers chosen. A coherent colour palette, creams and tans, for example, with a few torn pieces of printed text, produces a background that reads as a unified field. Mixing too many colours or contrasts makes the background compete with the elements laid on top.

items.name: Torn paper pieces, cartridge, old book pages, printed text, tissue paper, washi kozo
items.qty: a pile of various sizes and weights
items.substitutions: Old newspapers, maps, music scores, envelopes, anything printed or textured

items.name: PVA, diluted 2:1 (two parts PVA to one part water)

content.text: Tear all pieces before beginning to apply. Work across the grain if possible, machine-made paper tears more cleanly along the grain; tearing across the grain produces a softer, more feathered
. A mix of small (25 to 50 mm) and medium (50 to 100 mm) pieces works better than uniform sizes.
Brush diluted PVA onto the base sheet (the journal page or a piece of heavy cartridge). While still wet, press the first layer of torn pieces onto the surface, overlapping slightly.
over the first layer while still wet. This adheres the top edge of each piece and seals it for the next layer.
Add a second layer, placing pieces to cover gaps and vary the texture. Brush PVA over again.
Allow to dry completely flat. The PVA will dry transparent and slightly flexible. Weight the page if it cockles.
Once dry, the background surface is ready for stamping, writing, and adhering photographs or embellishments over the top.

items.symptom: The page cockles badly when dry
items.cause: Too much wet PVA applied; thin base sheet.
items.fix: Use a heavier base sheet (220 gsm minimum). Apply PVA sparingly, the torn pieces should be damp, not saturated. Press under a weight while drying.

items.symptom: The background looks muddy rather than layered
items.cause: Too many different paper colours used; papers are too dark.
items.fix: Restrict the palette to two or three tones. Use mostly light and mid-toned papers; strong darks read as mistakes rather than texture.
